content:
I am caching ref-counted objects in a lookup tree. When dropping ref-counts I generally do not want to lock the lookup-tree. However, when possibly dropping the last ref-count, I want to lock the tree and try again. I currently don't see a way to do this with Arc.

On standard Rust I would put Weak-refs into the lookup tree, but cannot do that with Arc. So I am looking for Arc::drop_if_more_than(self, n: usize) -> Option<Self>, which drops a reference if the number of references is higher than n. This would allow me to drop references without taking the lock, but retrying with the lock whenever it _might_ be the second to last. Inside of the lock I drop the reference, and if it then drops to 1 I can release the lookup entry as well.

I think this is semi-common with refcount_t, but was wondering whether you have better ideas with Arc, or if this would be ok to add?
There is a method to handle 1->0 refcount drop specially. For n > 1 there is no method for it.
why do you need it for second-to-last instead of just the last refcount?
I mean, I could just re-use the reference the lookup-tree owns, but it is kinda awkward when this spans multiple APIs or modules.
Emulating weak refs with drop_if_more_than is not a good idea IMO

I am not emulating weak refs. I am dropping a shared ref, if all unique refs were dropped. But even if you call it "emulating weak refs", I would appreciate if you elaborate "is not a good idea IMO". Not sure what to do with that.

Speaking of into_unique_or_drop(): How is this meant to work in this case? Lets say I make the lookup-tree not own a ref, so I link objects when creating them, and unlink when destroying them. When I call into_unique_or_drop() and get a UniqueArc, I still have to take the lock and then unlink the object. If another thread races this and gets the lock before, they might acquire a ref through lookup even though the original thread holds a UniqueArc. That is not safe.
I can make it work with drop_unless_last(). So very much like into_unique_or_drop(), but returning an Arc instead of UniqueArc.
ok, I can see how drop_if_more_than(2) does actually solve your problem, though weak refs also would solve it.

Btw., I came up with the following patch. This basically mirrors what kref_put_mutex() is doing.

Due to the design of the lookup tree, it means I still have to create a fake Arc and store it in the lookup tree, even though I do not increase the ref-count. I hide the Arc from public access, but still kinda awkward, since it would technically break existing calls on Arc like into_unique_or_drop().

I can also send it to the ML if that is preferred.

From 4b2ed52226cd88a4ee09101bc5735988f4037989 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: David Rheinsberg <david@readahead.eu>
Date: Thu, 12 Mar 2026 14:03:54 +0100
Subject: [PATCH] rust/sync: add Arc::drop_unless_unique()

Introduce `Arc::drop_unless_unique()`, a rust version of
`refcount_dec_not_one()`.

It is very common to store an object with `struct kref` (or
`refcount_t`) in a locked lookup-tree, but without the tree actually
owning a reference. Instead, whenever the object is destroyed, it
should be removed from the lookup tree.

The problem is, this requires taking the lock whenever releasing a
reference. Otherwise, a racing lookup might see a ref-count of 0. But
this is needlessly expensive.

One solution provided by `struct kref` is `kref_put_mutex()`. This drops
a reference, unless it is the last reference. In that case it takes a
mutex and tries again. If it no longer is the last reference, it simply
unlocks and returns. But if it dropped the last reference, it calls the
destructor with the lock held.

This entire technique is built around `refcount_dec_not_one()`. Expose
this exact feature in Rust, so it can be used to get the same effect as
`kref_put_mutex()`.

Signed-off-by: David Rheinsberg <david@readahead.eu>
---
 rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs      | 21 +++++++++++++++++++++
 rust/kernel/sync/refcount.rs | 16 ++++++++++++++++
 2 files changed, 37 insertions(+)

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs b/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs
index 921e19333b89..ead021a0dd5d 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs
@@ -368,6 +368,27 @@ pub fn into_unique_or_drop(this: Self) -> Option<Pin<UniqueArc<T>>> {
             None
         }
     }
+
+    /// Drop this [`Arc`] unless it is the last reference.
+    ///
+    /// When this is the last reference to the `Arc`, it is returned as `Some`
+    /// unmodified. Otherwise, the `Arc` is dropped and [`None`] is returned.
+    ///
+    /// This function will never release the last reference to the object, and
+    /// as such never call its destructor.
+    pub fn drop_unless_unique(this: Self) -> Option<Self> {
+        let this = ManuallyDrop::new(this);
+
+        // SAFETY: We own a refcount, so the pointer is still valid.
+        if unsafe { this.ptr.as_ref() }.refcount.dec_not_one() {
+            // A single ref-count was dropped, but it was not the last. We
+            // manually drop `this` without invoking `Drop`.
+            None
+        } else {
+            // This was a no-op, return the reference to the caller.
+            Some(ManuallyDrop::into_inner(this))
+        }
+    }
 }

 // SAFETY: The pointer returned by `into_foreign` was originally allocated as an
diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync/refcount.rs b/rust/kernel/sync/refcount.rs
index 6c7ae8b05a0b..2a65b3e3f961 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync/refcount.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync/refcount.rs
@@ -105,6 +105,22 @@ pub fn dec_and_test(&self) -> bool {
         // SAFETY: `self.as_ptr()` is valid.
         unsafe { bindings::refcount_dec_and_test(self.as_ptr()) }
     }
+
+    /// Decrement a refcount if it is not 1.
+    ///
+    /// It will `WARN` on underflow and fail to decrement when saturated.
+    ///
+    /// Provides release memory ordering when succeeding in decrementing the
+    /// refcount.
+    ///
+    /// Returns true if the decrement operation succeeded, false if the
+    /// decrement operation was skipped as the refcount is 1.
+    #[inline]
+    #[must_use = "refcount release is conditional and must be checked"]
+    pub fn dec_not_one(&self) -> bool {
+        // SAFETY: `self.as_ptr()` is valid.
+        unsafe { bindings::refcount_dec_not_one(self.as_ptr()) }
+    }
 }

 // SAFETY: `refcount_t` is thread-safe.
--
2.53.0
